title: A Warning Is Not a Decision
context: The Expenses squad is reviewing the first end-to-end claim flow with Product, Payroll, UX, and Operations. Everyone wants a cleaner approval journey, but there is pressure to keep phase one contained and avoid colliding with other payroll roadmap items. You are the solution architect. The Director of Product is chairing.

questions.prompt: The UX Lead says: "Out-of-policy values should be blocked at the UI. If the system knows a value is wrong, why let it travel through the workflow at all? We should enforce policy as early as possible." The Operations Lead points out that managers still need visibility of unusual claims and that some policies have configurable thresholds. How should you respond?

options.text: The UX Lead is right that early enforcement is cleaner. If the system already has the policy rules at the client layer, blocking at submission avoids unnecessary workflow processing and keeps invalid data out of the system entirely. We can always add exceptions later if managers request them.
options.explanation: This places enforcement logic in the wrong layer. Hard UI blocks assume every policy rule is binary and unconditional, but the broader system includes configurable auto-accept and auto-reject rules, manager visibility of out-of-policy claims, and the ability to override in defined circumstances. Moving all enforcement to the UI collapses several distinct concerns into a single decision point that cannot accommodate downstream configuration.

options.text: Policy enforcement should be distributed across the right layers rather than collapsed into the UI. At submission time, the employee should get a warning and a link to the relevant policy detail, which is a guidance concern. Actual acceptance or rejection should be driven by configurable rules downstream, where the sysadmin has set the auto-accept, auto-reject, or manual-review behaviour. The UI warns; the workflow decides. That separation lets us change enforcement behaviour through configuration without rewriting UI logic.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it separates guidance from enforcement and places each in the appropriate architectural layer. The UI handles user-facing warnings. The workflow engine handles configurable policy decisions. This separation means enforcement behaviour can be changed by sysadmins through configuration rather than requiring code changes at the client layer.

options.text: We should skip UI-level validation entirely and let the workflow engine handle all policy enforcement after submission. Warnings at the form level add complexity to the client and might confuse employees who do not understand the policy thresholds. It is simpler to let claims flow through and have the system accept or reject them based on the configured rules.
options.explanation: This removes a required part of the employee journey. The employee should be warned at the point of entry when they type an out-of-policy value, with a link to the relevant policy detail. Removing that feedback and making the employee wait for a post-submission decision misses the guidance intent of the feature.

questions.prompt: The Operations Lead says: "To simplify the MVP, let us make every claim go through the same manual path. No auto-approvals, no auto-rejections, just managers reviewing everything." The Sysadmin representative objects that this removes a major efficiency gain. How should you respond?

options.text: That simplification is worth it. Manual review of every claim is the safest first version because we avoid policy edge cases and do not need any admin configuration beyond basic submission and approval.
options.explanation: While it sounds safer, it drops a stated requirement. The setup section explicitly says the sysadmin should be able to configure the system so that certain claims are auto-rejected or auto-approved.

options.text: I would keep configurable automation in scope. The PRD explicitly gives sysadmins the ability to set certain claims to auto-accept or auto-reject, and that matters because the feature is meant to reduce manual effort, not just digitise it. We can constrain the initial rule set if needed, but removing the capability entirely would miss the requirement.
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. It protects an explicit setup capability while still leaving room to limit how ambitious the first rule catalogue is.

options.text: We can meet both needs by auto-approving everything that looks in policy and only routing suspicious claims to managers. That way we maximise speed in the first release and do not need sysadmin configuration at all.
options.explanation: This replaces admin-configurable behaviour with a hard-coded platform rule. The requirement is specifically that the sysadmin should be able to configure whether claims are auto-approved or auto-rejected.

questions.prompt: The Payroll Product Lead says: "While we are touching approvals, this is the moment to redesign the whole authorisation route inside Expenses. Let us make the module support its own configurable approval chain instead of waiting on Payroll." How should you respond?

options.text: That is the right move because approvals are central to the Expenses experience, and relying on another team's roadmap will only slow us down. If we own the authorisation route in Expenses, we control the end-to-end journey.
options.explanation: This conflicts with the epic-level scope statement. The Expenses module is not meant to introduce changes to the existing employee-to-line-manager authorisation route in this phase; enhanced configurable workflows are being delivered by Payroll separately.

options.text: I would keep the scope boundary intact. The current plan is that Expenses relies on the existing employee-to-line-manager route and then benefits from the enhanced configurable workflows Payroll is delivering later. If we try to build a separate authorisation engine inside Expenses now, we create overlap, delay the module, and move outside the stated release scope.
options.explanation: This is the correct response. It uses the explicit epic boundary to avoid scope creep and duplicate workflow capability inside the module.

options.text: We should do a temporary approval engine in Expenses for phase one and then decommission it once Payroll delivers the richer workflow capability. That gives us independence now without locking us into a permanent architecture.
options.explanation: Temporary workflow engines have a habit of becoming permanent. More importantly, this still violates the stated scope boundary that Expenses is not changing the authorisation route in this phase.

questions.prompt: The Engineering Manager says: "Multi-job selection adds complexity to the domain model. Every claim needs a job reference, validation against assignment data, and downstream routing to the correct payroll context. For simplicity, can we just default to the primary assignment and avoid that complexity?" How should you respond?

options.text: That simplification is safe for phase one. The primary assignment will be correct for most employees, and the few multi-job cases can be corrected manually by payroll. Adding a job-selection step to the claim model complicates the data flow for a minority case that can be handled operationally.
options.explanation: Silently defaulting to the primary assignment creates a data correctness problem that surfaces late. Expenses claimed against the wrong job will flow into the wrong payroll context, affect the wrong cost centre, and produce incorrect reporting. The requirement calls out multi-job selection explicitly because downstream correctness depends on the claim being associated with the right employment context from the start.

options.text: The claim domain model needs to carry the correct employment context because downstream systems depend on it for payroll processing, cost allocation, and reporting. If an employee has more than one job, the claim should capture which job the expense relates to. We can keep the interaction lightweight by defaulting to a likely option and only surfacing the choice when multiple assignments exist, but the data model must support the association rather than assuming it.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it frames the requirement as a domain model concern rather than a UI concern. The architectural point is that the claim entity must carry a valid employment context reference because payroll processing, cost allocation, and reporting all depend on it. The UX can be optimised, but the model cannot omit the relationship.

options.text: We should resolve the job context automatically by inferring it from the expense category and the employee's assignment history. If we can match the claim type to the most likely job, the employee never has to make the selection and the model still carries the correct reference.
options.explanation: Automated inference without employee confirmation introduces a different kind of correctness risk. The system may infer incorrectly, and there is no verification step to catch the mistake. The employee is the person who knows which job the expense relates to, so the selection belongs with them.

questions.prompt: The Product Manager says: "To keep the claim model simple, rejected claims should be terminal. If the employee wants to try again, they create a new claim. Supporting edit-and-resubmit on a rejected claim means the claim entity has to support state transitions back from rejected to submitted, which complicates the state machine and the audit trail." How should you respond?

options.text: A terminal rejected state is the cleanest model. Each claim has a linear lifecycle: draft, submitted, approved or rejected. Allowing re-entry from rejected to submitted adds a loop that complicates state management, event history, and reporting. A new claim referencing the original is simpler to implement and easier to audit.
options.explanation: While a simpler state machine is attractive, forcing the employee to recreate a claim from scratch after rejection adds friction the module is designed to eliminate. The requirement explicitly calls for the employee to be able to edit and resubmit a rejected claim. The state machine needs to accommodate that transition, and the audit trail should capture each version, but that is a solvable design problem rather than a reason to remove the capability.

options.text: The claim lifecycle needs to support re-entry from rejected to a draft or resubmitted state because that is part of the required employee journey. The state machine is slightly more complex, but it is well-understood: rejected claims transition to an editable state, the employee modifies and resubmits, and the audit trail records each version. Forcing a new claim discards the history and the context of the original submission, which makes the approval trail harder to follow, not easier.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it treats the state machine as a design problem to solve rather than a reason to cut the feature. The architectural point is that a claim's audit trail is richer and more traceable when edits are recorded against the same entity rather than scattered across unrelated claims. The lifecycle loop is a normal pattern for approval workflows.

options.text: We should avoid the issue by replacing rejection with a request-changes status. The manager annotates the claim, and the employee edits it in place without a formal rejection state. That keeps the state machine linear while still letting the employee correct the claim.
options.explanation: Redefining rejection as request-changes alters the workflow semantics rather than addressing the requirement. The system needs to handle claims that are genuinely rejected and notify the employee accordingly. A request-changes status may be a useful addition, but it does not replace the need for a rejection state and the ability to recover from it.
